---
pagetitle: "Projects · Simon Hamra"
description: "Three labor market analytics projects built on Lightcast job posting data with PySpark, Spark SQL and machine learning."
page-layout: custom
---

::: {.page-intro}
::: {.wrap}
<span class="eyebrow">Projects</span>

# Three course assignments.

::: {.lead}
These are the three graded projects from my AD688 Applied Business Analytics course at Boston University. All three run on the same large Lightcast job posting dataset, and each one targets a different skill: modeling the data in SQL, cleaning and charting it at scale, and building machine learning models on it. I kept the honest numbers, including the parts that were hard. Each case links to the assignment repository with the full notebook.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.section}
::: {.wrap}

<!-- ================= A02 ================= -->
::: {#a02 .case}
::: {.case__grid}
::: {.case__text}
<span class="eyebrow">Assignment 02 · Spark SQL · Data modeling</span>

## A job market in SQL

I turned one flat Lightcast file into a small data warehouse, then answered four real questions about pay and hiring in Spark SQL.

<dl class="dl">
<div><dt>Problem</dt><dd>A single wide table is hard to query and full of repeated values. I wanted clean, joinable tables and a few clear answers about pay and hiring.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Constraints</dt><dd>The same large Lightcast file, so I built and queried everything in Spark SQL instead of loading it into memory.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Approach</dt><dd>I split the data into dimension tables for <strong>companies, industries and locations</strong>, each with its own surrogate key, then joined them back into one fact table of postings. That is a <strong>star schema</strong>. On top of it I wrote four queries: median pay by specialized occupation inside the Technology industry (NAICS 518210), the top five remote employers in California, month-by-month California posting volume, and average pay across twelve major US metros.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Tradeoff</dt><dd>I could have kept one flat table and queried it directly, which is faster to set up. I chose the star schema because the joins stay clean, the dimension tables are reusable, and it is closer to how a real data warehouse works.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Result</dt><dd><strong>1 star schema, 4 queries.</strong> Pay is highest in expensive metros like San Francisco and Seattle, remote hiring concentrates in a few large employers, and California posting volume dips over the summer then recovers toward the end of the year.</dd></div>
</dl>

<div class="tags"><span class="tag">PySpark</span><span class="tag">Spark SQL</span><span class="tag">Star schema</span><span class="tag">Joins</span><span class="tag">seaborn</span><span class="tag">matplotlib</span></div>

::: {.btn-row}
[Repository &amp; notebook](https://github.com/AD688-SP26/a02-sum26-simonhamra){.btn .btn--ghost}
:::
:::
::: {.case__fig}
![](assets/charts/pay_by_state.png)
<figcaption>Median advertised salary by state. The pay gap between the top states and the rest is large.</figcaption>
:::
:::
:::

<!-- ================= A03 ================= -->
::: {#a03 .case}
::: {.case__grid}
::: {.case__text}
<span class="eyebrow">Assignment 03 · PySpark · Visualization</span>

## Salary insights at scale

I took a 700 MB Lightcast file, cleaned it in Spark, and turned it into clear pictures of who gets paid what.

<dl class="dl">
<div><dt>Problem</dt><dd>The file holds tens of thousands of job postings, but it is messy. Salary, experience and education all load as text, and most rows do not even list a salary. I needed clean, honest numbers I could chart.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Constraints</dt><dd>The raw file is close to <strong>700 MB</strong>, too big to open in pandas on a laptop. So all the heavy work, loading, casting, medians and grouping, had to stay in Spark. I only pulled small, already-aggregated tables into pandas right before drawing each chart.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Approach</dt><dd>I cast the salary and experience columns to numbers, computed medians in Spark, and filled missing salaries with a careful midpoint rule, keeping a separate copy of the real observed salary so a reported median never uses a filled value. Then I built five visuals on a custom Plotly theme: a salary-floor box plot by industry and employment type, a median-pay bubble chart by occupation, experience-versus-pay scatters split by education and by remote type, and an overlaid salary histogram.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Tradeoff</dt><dd>I had to choose between dropping rows with no salary or filling them. Dropping throws away most of the data; filling keeps it but can hide the truth. My answer was to do both: fill for completeness, but always use the observed salary for any real median.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Result</dt><dd><strong>72,498 postings</strong> cleaned and charted. Full-time roles pay more, remote and hybrid roles lean higher, and the best-paid occupation reaches about <strong>$158k</strong> while the most common roles sit near $95k to $126k.</dd></div>
</dl>

<div class="tags"><span class="tag">PySpark</span><span class="tag">pandas</span><span class="tag">Plotly</span><span class="tag">Seaborn</span><span class="tag">Quarto</span></div>

::: {.btn-row}
[Repository &amp; notebook](https://github.com/AD688-SP26/a03-sum26-simonhamra){.btn .btn--ghost}
:::
:::
::: {.case__fig}
![](assets/charts/pay_by_occupation.png)
<figcaption>Median advertised salary by occupation, using real observed pay only. The engineering heavy roles sit at the top.</figcaption>
:::
:::
:::

<!-- ================= A05 ================= -->
::: {#a05 .case}
::: {.case__grid}
::: {.case__text}
<span class="eyebrow">Assignment 05 · Machine learning</span>

## Predicting salary, and a trap

I trained three models to predict salary, and hit a classic trap that stops a model from giving any statistics.

<dl class="dl">
<div><dt>Problem</dt><dd>I wanted to predict salary from experience, education, posting duration and how the role is delivered, and also explain which feature matters most.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Constraints</dt><dd>After I required a real salary plus every feature present, only about <strong>2,000 rows</strong> were left out of the roughly 30,000 that had a salary. Small, but every value is real and nothing is filled in.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Approach</dt><dd>I built a Spark ML pipeline with indexing, one-hot encoding and a feature vector, then fit a generalized linear model, a polynomial model with a squared experience term, and a random forest with 200 trees and depth 8. The data was split 80 / 20 with a fixed seed, and I compared the models on RMSE, R², AIC and BIC.</dd></div>
<div><dt>The trap</dt><dd>Spark gave me coefficients but refused the standard errors, so no p-values and no confidence intervals. The cause was <strong>multicollinearity</strong>: the minimum and maximum experience columns were identical in every row, correlation exactly <strong>1.0</strong>. I dropped one column and the statistics came back.</dd></div>
<div><dt>Result</dt><dd>Each extra year of experience is worth about <strong>$8,000</strong>, and that effect is strong. The random forest was the most accurate model, but the linear model explains the story best. Experience carries about <strong>57%</strong> of the importance, far ahead of education and industry.</dd></div>
</dl>

<div class="tags"><span class="tag">Spark ML</span><span class="tag">Random forest</span><span class="tag">GLR</span><span class="tag">Polynomial</span><span class="tag">Stats</span></div>

::: {.btn-row}
[Repository &amp; notebook](https://github.com/AD688-SP26/a05-sum26-simonhamra){.btn .btn--ghost}
:::
:::
::: {.case__fig}
![](assets/charts/salary_drivers.png)
<figcaption>A random forest on the Lightcast features. Years of experience is by far the strongest driver of salary.</figcaption>
:::
:::
:::
:::
